romania.title: Care este starea băiețelului de cinci ani salvat din pădure. Afecțiunea descoperită de medici, pentru care va rămâne internat
romania.summary: Alexandru, băiatul de cinci ani găsit miercuri într-o zonă de pădure după ce dispăruse, acum două zile, din localitatea sibiană Sebeșu de Jos, a ajuns la Spitalul de Pediatrie din Sibiu. Cu toate că starea lui generală „este satisfăcătoare”, el va rămâne internat din cauza unei afecțiuni descoperite de medici, a explicat pentru HotNews dr. …

romania.title: 😂 Restructurarea STB provoacă haos după ce mai mulți vatmani au fost dați afară din mers
romania.summary: Graba cu care primăria Capitalei a pus în practică restructurarea STB a creat haos în traficul din oraș. Dintr-un exces de zel greu de înțeles, angajații primăriei au dat afară mai mulți vatmani chiar în timp ce aceștia se aflau la manșeta tramvaiului. Martorii spun că unii vatmani nici n-au apucat să înțeleagă ce li […] The post Restructurarea STB provoacă haos după ce mai mulți vatmani au fost dați afară din mers appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Primăria Sectorului 2 a primit undă verde să-și înființeze o companie de infrastructură și un club sportiv
romania.summary: Pe lângă lucrările și intervențiile de infrastructură în Sectorul 2, noua companie va fi implicată în construirea de noi locuri de parcare, care vor putea fi folosite pe baza unui regulament aprobat de Consiliul Local, spune primarul Sectorului 2, Rareș Hopincă. Noua companie pentru lucrări de infrastructură ar urma să fie sub tutela Consiliului Local …

romania.title: Momentul în care copilul dispărut în Sibiu a fost găsit: „L-am văzut într-un luminiș, iar el ne-a făcut cu mâna”
romania.summary: Mihail Soare, unul dintre salvatorii implicați în operațiunea de căutare a copilului dispărut în județul Sibiu, a povestit momentul în care băiatul a fost găsit într-un luminiș din pădure, după ore întregi de căutări. Copilul din localitatea Sebeșu de Jos, căutat de sute de persoane după dispariția sa de luni, a fost găsit în viață la aproximativ doi kilometri de locul în care plecase de lângă tatăl său.

romania.title: „Ne-a făcut cu mâna”. Momentul în care a fost găsit copilul de 5 ani, după zeci de ore de căutări, relatat de unul dintre salvatori / Ce spun părinții lui Alexandru
romania.summary: „A fost un moment emoționant, nu am crezut că o să trăiesc vreodată așa ceva”, spune Mihail Soare, unul dintre salvatorii implicați în operațiunea de căutare a băiețelului din Sibiu căutat timp de două zile de sute de polițiști, jandarmi și voluntari. Copilul a fost găsit într-o pădure, într-un loc „total nemarcat, neumblat”, spun salvamontiștii. …

romania.title: Tur ghidat organizat de Fundația Calea Victoriei | Descoperă Bucureştiul Art Deco şi arhitectura modernistă
romania.summary: Fundația Calea Victoriei organizează luni, 25 mai 2026, de la ora 18:00, un nou tur ghidat dedicat Bucureștiului interbelic și patrimoniului său Art Deco și modernist, alături de Ovidiu Neacșu… Articolul Tur ghidat organizat de Fundația Calea Victoriei | Descoperă Bucureştiul Art Deco şi arhitectura modernistă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Probele s-au desfășurat în timpul orelor de curs și au avut o durată de 45 de minute.

Potrivit Poliţiei Locale bruxelleze, un român în vârstă de 39 de ani a fost surprins în timp ce îşi spăla maşina folosind un furtun pe care îl conectase clandestin la […] The post Scandal în Bruxelles după ce un român a mufat un furtun la Manneken Pis ca să-şi spele maşina appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

medical.title: Future cancer therapy could use immunity to clean up damaged DNA by modulating AUF1
medical.summary: Researchers have identified a pathway that triggers an immune response in cells with defective DNA repair. In particular, the authors of a new paper demonstrated how the downregulation of AUF1 impairs DNA repair, followed by activation of the immune response due to the presence of R-loops in the cytoplasm. This represents an important finding for the development of new therapeutic strategies aimed at modulating the immune response upon DNA damage.

medical.title: Teen depression linked with higher substance use rates
medical.summary: Nearly 1 in 5 U.S. adolescents experienced depression between 2021 and 2023, and those teens were significantly more likely to use substances such as alcohol, marijuana and opioids, according to a new national study.

medical.title: Cannabis: It's medicine if you're rich enough, a crime if you're not
medical.summary: In Britain, whether cannabis is treated as medicine or a crime may depend less on medical need than on the ability to pay. In 2018, the UK government changed drug policy, allowing specialist doctors to prescribe cannabis-based medicinal products.

medical.title: Analyzing individual food patterns for healthier gut microbiomes
medical.summary: University of Minnesota researchers are developing a personalized approach to track how individual food choices impact the gut microbiome. Researchers from the College of Food, Agricultural and Natural Resource Sciences and The Hormel Institute are creating an algorithm that uses mobile health technologies and artificial intelligence to collect and analyze large amounts of dietary information to better understand the bacteria living in a person's gut.

medical.title: Study finds major gap in pain diagnosis
medical.summary: Millions of Americans experience pain severe enough to interfere with daily life but never receive a medical diagnosis, according to a new study from The University of Texas at Arlington. Feinuo Sun, assistant professor of kinesiology, led the study that introduced the term "undiagnosed pain," defined as "self-reported pain without any formal diagnosis of an underlying condition."

medical.title: Magnetic brain stimulation cuts alcohol cravings in 200-person addiction trial
medical.summary: A storyteller with a passion for popular education. Head of one of Linköping University's strongest research environments. And a physician. Markus Heilig researches the biology of the brain in addiction, and always considers the whole person.

medical.title: Europe advances genomic surveillance of antimicrobial resistance with multi-country study
medical.summary: A major European survey marks a significant milestone in the effort to control antimicrobial resistance (AMR), providing the most comprehensive genomic picture to date of carbapenem- and/or colistin-resistant Enterobacterales (CCRE) across hospitals in Europe. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales are critical priority pathogens posing a high risk for public health and patient safety, as they cause difficult-to-treat infections with limited available treatment options and an insufficient development pipeline for new antibiotics.

medical.title: Most weight loss achieved using injectable obesity drugs can be maintained by switching to orforglipron, study shows
medical.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May) and published in Nature Medicine shows that most weight loss achieved using the injectable obesity drugs tirzepatide or semaglutide can be maintained by switching to daily oral orforglipron treatment.

medical.title: Continuing tirzepatide at full dose helps preserve weight loss over 112 weeks
medical.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO 2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May) and published in The Lancet shows that people who have lost weight using the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of tirzepatide are seven times more likely to maintain that weight loss by continuing on MTD than those who stop treatment, while those who switch to a lower dose of 5 mg are four times more likely to maintain weight loss than those who stop.

medical.title: A 13-gene panel may help predict response to chemotherapy in triple-negative breast cancer
medical.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center have characterized cancer cell-specific features in the tumor microenvironment (TME) of early-stage triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) tissues, identifying specific macrophage subtypes associated with chemotherapy response.

medical.title: Non-coding gene is linked to core social and behavioral traits in autism
medical.summary: A long-overlooked stretch of the human genome appears to play a distinct role in shaping the social and stereotypic repetitive behaviors that define autism spectrum disorder (ASD), without affecting learning or other cognitive abilities, according to a study published in Nature.

medical.title: Pediatric surgery program cuts opioid use by 56%
medical.summary: A 21-element recovery program for children undergoing gastrointestinal surgery reduced opioid use during hospitalization by 56%, according to a large clinical trial led by Northwestern University and Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago.

medical.title: Forgotten and isolated: 1.8 million people still vulnerable to COVID-19 face a mental health crisis
medical.summary: Levels of depression and anxiety remain high and largely unrecognized among an estimated 1.8 million clinically vulnerable people in the UK, many of whom continue to live significantly restricted lives to protect them from COVID 19, according to new research from the University of Bath.

medical.title: This daily habit could lower dementia risk by 35%, scientists say
medical.summary: A huge long-term study found that drinking two to three cups of coffee a day was linked to a much lower risk of dementia, especially before age 75. Researchers say caffeine may help keep brain cells active while reducing inflammation and harmful plaque buildup associated with Alzheimer’s disease. But more coffee wasn’t better — the protective effect appeared to level off after moderate intake.

medical.title: Scientists discover a mysterious silicone pollutant that may be everywhere
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered unexpectedly high levels of silicone-based pollutants called methylsiloxanes floating through the atmosphere across cities, rural regions, and even forests. Much of the pollution appears to come from vehicle emissions, likely linked to engine oil additives that survive combustion and escape into the air. Scientists say humans may inhale more of these compounds daily than other notorious pollutants like PFAS or microplastics.

medical.title: Your “um” and pauses could reveal early dementia risk
medical.summary: The little pauses, “ums,” and moments when you struggle to find the right word may reveal far more about your brain than anyone realized. Researchers discovered that everyday speech patterns are closely tied to executive function — the mental system that powers memory, planning, focus, and flexible thinking. By using AI to analyze natural conversations, the team found they could predict cognitive performance with surprising accuracy, potentially opening the door to simple speech-based tools that could detect early signs of dementia long before traditional testing does.

medical.title: Scientists found the brain doesn’t start blank, it starts full
medical.summary: The brain’s memory center may begin life more like a crowded web than an empty canvas. Researchers discovered that early neural networks in the hippocampus are dense and seemingly random, then become more organized by shedding connections over time. This pruning process creates a faster, more efficient system for linking experiences and forming memories. It challenges the idea that the brain starts from scratch.

medical.title: Are your memories real? Physicists revisit the Boltzmann brain paradox
medical.summary: A new analysis of the “Boltzmann brain” paradox suggests our memories and sense of reality could, in theory, be random illusions born from cosmic chaos. By uncovering circular reasoning in how physicists think about time and entropy, the study raises fresh doubts about what we can truly know about the past.

science.title: Study reveals how, when political parties communicate with citizens in multiple languages
science.summary: A new Washington University in St. Louis' study takes on the Herculean task of studying the linguistic choices of more than 800 political parties from 87 democracies around the world, including the United States.

science.title: NASA bets big on nuclear engines to cut journey times to Mars
science.summary: Nasa is developing ways to use nuclear power to send spacecraft to their destinations. Nuclear propulsion could greatly reduce the journey time to Mars, perhaps cutting a voyage of more than six months to three or four months.

science.title: Twisted liquid-crystal structures unlock on-demand skyrmions at room temperature
science.summary: Researchers have recently found a new way to summon useful structures in magnetic materials using light, heat, and electric fields. This new method, described in a new study published in Physical Review Letters, may lead to more energy-efficient and flexible technologies for data storage and optical devices.

science.title: Bee magnetism appears far more widespread than expected across 120 species
science.summary: As married research professors at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, Dustin Gilbert and Anne Murray often discuss their work once they get home each night. Their fields of study rarely crossover. That changed six years ago, however, and it was insects that sparked the intersection.

science.title: Methane fingerprints sharpen global emissions map, pointing to China, India and Central Africa
science.summary: Atmospheric methane levels have surged to record highs in recent years and are projected to increase by as much as 13% by 2030, according to a report from the Climate & Clean Air Coalition. As scientists work to better understand what is driving this rise, a new collaborative study published in Nature Communications used methane isotopologues to trace where recent emissions originate and how they are changing around the world.

science.title: Most people don't know what they don't know, but think they do
science.summary: Do you know what the Apple logo looks like? Chances are, you think you do. It's ubiquitous and iconic. How could you not know it? But when tested, it turns out very few people can remember all the features of the logo. One study of 85 people found that only about half could pick the correct logo out of a lineup of similar ones. And only one person could correctly draw it.

science.title: Bottom trawling is scraping oceans of wildlife
science.summary: Bottom trawlers extract one-quarter of the world's fisheries catches by weight and raise significant ecological, economic and social concerns. Given that, you'd think there would be an answer to basic questions in fisheries: how many fish species are being caught, and what are they?

science.title: Mostly empty foam overturns assumptions of electron beam stopping
science.summary: When physicists fire beams of fast electrons at materials, they often need to know exactly how much energy those electrons will lose as they travel through. Through new research published in Physical Review Letters, a team led by Ke Jiang at Shenzhen Technology University in China has found that porous, mostly empty foam materials can stop high-current electron beams far more effectively than denser materials—overturning many previous assumptions about how these beams interact with solid materials.

science.title: Beluga calls deciphered to bolster conservation efforts
science.summary: Alaska's Cook Inlet was home to nearly 1,300 beluga whales in the late 1970s, but today the population hovers around 300. Despite almost two decades of recovery work, the whales aren't bouncing back. The Cook Inlet belugas are likely struggling under multiple pressures, including increasing human noise. Researchers are working on deciphering whale-whale communication to better account for the impact of noise on this vulnerable population.

science.title: Why global businesses are becoming quietly entwined with the military
science.summary: Big corporations are not just influenced by governments anymore—they are being increasingly influenced by the military, according to a new study from the University of Surrey. The research, published in the Journal of World Business, shows many multinational firms now build competitive advantage through structured relationships with military institutions, from hiring veterans and using security contractors to accessing defense-funded innovations and protecting markets. Rather than being rare or controversial, this behavior is becoming routine in unstable global markets.

science.title: Climate change is reshaping Europe's protected areas, and managers are adapting
science.summary: New research shows how climate change is reshaping protected area management, though more funding and scientific knowledge are needed to facilitate the process. The Natura 2000 network, the world's largest network of protected areas, aims at protecting important species and habitats across the European Union. Its traditional approach to conservation has focused on maintaining ecosystems in their historical conditions, but in a world characterized by a changing climate, this may not be possible anymore, requiring adapting the conservation strategies.

science.title: Barbell 'whip' may shape Olympic lifts more than lifters realize
science.summary: In Olympic weightlifting, a single kilogram plate can be the difference between gold and silver. As much as possible, elite athletes must use everything they can to their advantage.

science.title: Predicting typhoon intensity using ocean surface temperatures
science.summary: Every year in the West Pacific, as summer ends and September rolls around, typhoons are not far behind. Typhoons are the most impactful extreme weather events affecting Japan and East Asia, and due to climate change, extremely strong typhoons are becoming more frequent. In order to adapt critical infrastructure to these massive storms and protect coastal areas, accurate accounting for their future impact is essential.

science.title: Laser treatment reshapes MOF pores, boosting CO₂ capture by up to 75%
science.summary: A research team led by Hee-jung Lee, senior researcher at Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS), in collaboration with Professor Sunghwan Park of Kyungpook National University and Professor Mingyu Kim of Yeungnam University, has developed a technology that enhances CO₂ adsorption performance in metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) by up to 75% through precise laser-based control of their internal structure.

science.title: A marine-inspired sunscreen ingredient made by E. coli
science.summary: How do fish survive relentless sunlight in the open waters without getting burned? They make their own natural sunscreen—and now, humans could be one step closer to using it too.

science.title: Identity traits sharply narrow who becomes friends or marries, model reveals
science.summary: Our personal identity is composed of many dimensions, such as age, gender, ethnic background, or socioeconomic status. A research team led by Fariba Karimi from the Institute of Human-Centered Computing at Graz University of Technology (TU Graz) and Samuel Martin-Gutierrez from the Complexity Science Hub developed the statistical computational model "MAPS" to calculate the influence of these factors on our social relationships.

science.title: SNOR protein provides 'all-clear' signal for dormant cells to resume normal operations
science.summary: It's a tough world for microbes. When resources grow limited and environments worsen, microbes have figured out ways to hunker down and go dormant until conditions improve.

science.title: 3D atomic rearrangement creates 40,000 quantum defects in 40 minutes
science.summary: It's been 37 years since scientists first demonstrated the ability to move single atoms, suggesting the possibility of designing materials atom by atom to customize their properties. Today there are several techniques that allow researchers to move individual atoms in order to give materials exotic quantum properties and improve our understanding of quantum behavior.

science.title: Fossil teeth from China uncover 400,000-year-old H. erectus ties to Denisovans
science.summary: Scientists from the Institute of Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoanthropology (IVPP) of the Chinese Academy of Sciences have uncovered new information suggesting a potential connection between Homo erectus and modern humans, while also developing new, less invasive paleoproteomics methods of fossil research.

science.title: AI generates first complete models of proteins in motion
science.summary: Many drug and antibody discovery pathways focus on intricately folded cell membrane proteins. When molecules of a drug candidate bind to these proteins, like a key going into a lock, they trigger chemical cascades that alter cellular behavior. Understanding how proteins fold and move is therefore essential for developing drugs that interact well with their targets.

science.title: Rivalry with neighboring groups may be a key driver of male size in primates
science.summary: In many primate species, males are much larger than their female counterparts, which is generally attributed to male competition for mates (sexual selection). But bigger bodies may not just be about alpha males defeating rivals. They could also come about because of competition between neighboring social groups, according to a new study published in the journal Biology Letters.

science.title: Scientists finally solve the 100-year mystery behind tough tires
science.summary: For nearly 100 years, reinforced rubber has powered everything from car tires to airplanes, yet scientists never fully understood why adding tiny particles of carbon black made rubber so incredibly strong. Now, researchers at the University of South Florida have finally cracked the mystery using massive computer simulations that took the equivalent of 15 years of computing time. They discovered that carbon black forces rubber to “fight against itself” when stretched, dramatically boosting its strength and durability.

science.title: Quantum breakthrough could revolutionize teleportation and computing
science.summary: Scientists in Japan have developed a new way to instantly detect elusive quantum “W states,” a major milestone for quantum technology. The breakthrough could help unlock faster quantum communication, teleportation, and powerful new computing systems.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Care este starea băiețelului de cinci ani salvat din pădure. Afecțiunea descoperită de medici, pentru care va rămâne internat
mix_items.summary: Alexandru, băiatul de cinci ani găsit miercuri într-o zonă de pădure după ce dispăruse, acum două zile, din localitatea sibiană Sebeșu de Jos, a ajuns la Spitalul de Pediatrie din Sibiu. Cu toate că starea lui generală „este satisfăcătoare”, el va rămâne internat din cauza unei afecțiuni descoperite de medici, a explicat pentru HotNews dr. …

mix_items.title: Primăria Sectorului 2 a primit undă verde să-și înființeze o companie de infrastructură și un club sportiv
mix_items.summary: Pe lângă lucrările și intervențiile de infrastructură în Sectorul 2, noua companie va fi implicată în construirea de noi locuri de parcare, care vor putea fi folosite pe baza unui regulament aprobat de Consiliul Local, spune primarul Sectorului 2, Rareș Hopincă. Noua companie pentru lucrări de infrastructură ar urma să fie sub tutela Consiliului Local …

mix_items.title: Momentul în care copilul dispărut în Sibiu a fost găsit: „L-am văzut într-un luminiș, iar el ne-a făcut cu mâna”
mix_items.summary: Mihail Soare, unul dintre salvatorii implicați în operațiunea de căutare a copilului dispărut în județul Sibiu, a povestit momentul în care băiatul a fost găsit într-un luminiș din pădure, după ore întregi de căutări. Copilul din localitatea Sebeșu de Jos, căutat de sute de persoane după dispariția sa de luni, a fost găsit în viață la aproximativ doi kilometri de locul în care plecase de lângă tatăl său.

mix_items.title: „Ne-a făcut cu mâna”. Momentul în care a fost găsit copilul de 5 ani, după zeci de ore de căutări, relatat de unul dintre salvatori / Ce spun părinții lui Alexandru
mix_items.summary: „A fost un moment emoționant, nu am crezut că o să trăiesc vreodată așa ceva”, spune Mihail Soare, unul dintre salvatorii implicați în operațiunea de căutare a băiețelului din Sibiu căutat timp de două zile de sute de polițiști, jandarmi și voluntari. Copilul a fost găsit într-o pădure, într-un loc „total nemarcat, neumblat”, spun salvamontiștii. …

mix_items.title: Tur ghidat organizat de Fundația Calea Victoriei | Descoperă Bucureştiul Art Deco şi arhitectura modernistă
mix_items.summary: Fundația Calea Victoriei organizează luni, 25 mai 2026, de la ora 18:00, un nou tur ghidat dedicat Bucureștiului interbelic și patrimoniului său Art Deco și modernist, alături de Ovidiu Neacșu… Articolul Tur ghidat organizat de Fundația Calea Victoriei | Descoperă Bucureştiul Art Deco şi arhitectura modernistă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Inspirat de Summitul B9, Nea Costel organizează în seara asta Summitul Bei 9
mix_items.summary: Summitul B9 care a dat peste cap traficul din Bucureşti e mic copil faţă de ce o să se întâmple diseară în birtul din Piaţa Miniş! Nea Costel, îndrăgitul lider al drojdierilor est-europeni, a convocat zeci de beţivi importanţi din întreaga lume pentru Summitul Bei 9. „Ne întâlnim în formula Bei 9 pentru a bea […] The post Inspirat de Summitul B9, Nea Costel organizează în seara asta Summitul Bei 9 appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Muzeul Național de Istorie Naturală „Grigore Antipa”” și Moon Bazar vă invită pe 16 și 17 mai 2026 la două zile dedicate copiilor, imaginației și distracției în aer liber, în grădina Muzeului Antipa!
mix_items.summary: Unde: Grădina Muzeului Antipa (Piața Victoriei) Pe cât: 16 – 17 mai 2026 10:00 – 20:00 Ce îi așteaptă pe cei mici? -Atelier creativ de confecționat corali Octyplay: copiii își… Articolul Muzeul Național de Istorie Naturală „Grigore Antipa”” și Moon Bazar vă invită pe 16 și 17 mai 2026 la două zile dedicate copiilor, imaginației și distracției în aer liber, în grădina Muzeului Antipa! apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Restructurarea STB provoacă haos după ce mai mulți vatmani au fost dați afară din mers
mix_items.summary: Graba cu care primăria Capitalei a pus în practică restructurarea STB a creat haos în traficul din oraș. Dintr-un exces de zel greu de înțeles, angajații primăriei au dat afară mai mulți vatmani chiar în timp ce aceștia se aflau la manșeta tramvaiului. Martorii spun că unii vatmani nici n-au apucat să înțeleagă ce li […] The post Restructurarea STB provoacă haos după ce mai mulți vatmani au fost dați afară din mers appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, revine în mijlocul orașelor din România
mix_items.summary: Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, revine în 2026 cu o nouă ediție a caravanei naționale care transformă spațiile urbane în terenuri temporare dedicate mișcării, sportului și jocului. Ajuns… Articolul Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, revine în mijlocul orașelor din România apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: AI Act & AI Competencies for Leaders: De la reglementare la gândire critică responsabilă
mix_items.summary: Liderii de business de la Cluj au participat la un eveniment organizat de GetFrankly și Legal Accelerators pentru a dezbate conformarea cu EU AI Act și competențele esențiale în era… Articolul AI Act & AI Competencies for Leaders: De la reglementare la gândire critică responsabilă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psihopedagog/Pedagog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: 😂 Scandal în Bruxelles după ce un român a mufat un furtun la Manneken Pis ca să-şi spele maşina
mix_items.summary: La nici două zile după ce simpaticul Manneken Pis a fost îmbrăcat în căluşar, belgienii au descoperit fără să vrea cealaltă latură a culturii noastre! Potrivit Poliţiei Locale bruxelleze, un român în vârstă de 39 de ani a fost surprins în timp ce îşi spăla maşina folosind un furtun pe care îl conectase clandestin la […] The post Scandal în Bruxelles după ce un român a mufat un furtun la Manneken Pis ca să-şi spele maşina appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agent telemarketing (team leader)
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Subiecte Evaluarea Națională 2026, clasa a II-a: Elevii au susținut proba de citire la Limbă și comunicare. Când se anunță rezultatele
mix_items.summary: Elevii de clasa a II-a au început în această săptămână Evaluarea Națională 2026, odată cu susținerea probei scrise la Limbă și comunicare, organizată marți, urmată miercuri de testarea competențelor de citire. Probele s-au desfășurat în timpul orelor de curs și au avut o durată de 45 de minute.

mix_items.title: Future cancer therapy could use immunity to clean up damaged DNA by modulating AUF1
mix_items.summary: Researchers have identified a pathway that triggers an immune response in cells with defective DNA repair. In particular, the authors of a new paper demonstrated how the downregulation of AUF1 impairs DNA repair, followed by activation of the immune response due to the presence of R-loops in the cytoplasm. This represents an important finding for the development of new therapeutic strategies aimed at modulating the immune response upon DNA damage.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Study reveals how, when political parties communicate with citizens in multiple languages
mix_items.summary: A new Washington University in St. Louis' study takes on the Herculean task of studying the linguistic choices of more than 800 political parties from 87 democracies around the world, including the United States.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Teen depression linked with higher substance use rates
mix_items.summary: Nearly 1 in 5 U.S. adolescents experienced depression between 2021 and 2023, and those teens were significantly more likely to use substances such as alcohol, marijuana and opioids, according to a new national study.

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: NASA bets big on nuclear engines to cut journey times to Mars
mix_items.summary: Nasa is developing ways to use nuclear power to send spacecraft to their destinations. Nuclear propulsion could greatly reduce the journey time to Mars, perhaps cutting a voyage of more than six months to three or four months.

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Twisted liquid-crystal structures unlock on-demand skyrmions at room temperature
mix_items.summary: Researchers have recently found a new way to summon useful structures in magnetic materials using light, heat, and electric fields. This new method, described in a new study published in Physical Review Letters, may lead to more energy-efficient and flexible technologies for data storage and optical devices.

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Bee magnetism appears far more widespread than expected across 120 species
mix_items.summary: As married research professors at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, Dustin Gilbert and Anne Murray often discuss their work once they get home each night. Their fields of study rarely crossover. That changed six years ago, however, and it was insects that sparked the intersection.

mix_items.title: Methane fingerprints sharpen global emissions map, pointing to China, India and Central Africa
mix_items.summary: Atmospheric methane levels have surged to record highs in recent years and are projected to increase by as much as 13% by 2030, according to a report from the Climate & Clean Air Coalition. As scientists work to better understand what is driving this rise, a new collaborative study published in Nature Communications used methane isotopologues to trace where recent emissions originate and how they are changing around the world.

mix_items.title: Most people don't know what they don't know, but think they do
mix_items.summary: Do you know what the Apple logo looks like? Chances are, you think you do. It's ubiquitous and iconic. How could you not know it? But when tested, it turns out very few people can remember all the features of the logo. One study of 85 people found that only about half could pick the correct logo out of a lineup of similar ones. And only one person could correctly draw it.

mix_items.title: Cannabis: It's medicine if you're rich enough, a crime if you're not
mix_items.summary: In Britain, whether cannabis is treated as medicine or a crime may depend less on medical need than on the ability to pay. In 2018, the UK government changed drug policy, allowing specialist doctors to prescribe cannabis-based medicinal products.

mix_items.title: Bottom trawling is scraping oceans of wildlife
mix_items.summary: Bottom trawlers extract one-quarter of the world's fisheries catches by weight and raise significant ecological, economic and social concerns. Given that, you'd think there would be an answer to basic questions in fisheries: how many fish species are being caught, and what are they?

mix_items.title: Analyzing individual food patterns for healthier gut microbiomes
mix_items.summary: University of Minnesota researchers are developing a personalized approach to track how individual food choices impact the gut microbiome. Researchers from the College of Food, Agricultural and Natural Resource Sciences and The Hormel Institute are creating an algorithm that uses mobile health technologies and artificial intelligence to collect and analyze large amounts of dietary information to better understand the bacteria living in a person's gut.

mix_items.title: Mostly empty foam overturns assumptions of electron beam stopping
mix_items.summary: When physicists fire beams of fast electrons at materials, they often need to know exactly how much energy those electrons will lose as they travel through. Through new research published in Physical Review Letters, a team led by Ke Jiang at Shenzhen Technology University in China has found that porous, mostly empty foam materials can stop high-current electron beams far more effectively than denser materials—overturning many previous assumptions about how these beams interact with solid materials.

mix_items.title: Study finds major gap in pain diagnosis
mix_items.summary: Millions of Americans experience pain severe enough to interfere with daily life but never receive a medical diagnosis, according to a new study from The University of Texas at Arlington. Feinuo Sun, assistant professor of kinesiology, led the study that introduced the term "undiagnosed pain," defined as "self-reported pain without any formal diagnosis of an underlying condition."

mix_items.title: Beluga calls deciphered to bolster conservation efforts
mix_items.summary: Alaska's Cook Inlet was home to nearly 1,300 beluga whales in the late 1970s, but today the population hovers around 300. Despite almost two decades of recovery work, the whales aren't bouncing back. The Cook Inlet belugas are likely struggling under multiple pressures, including increasing human noise. Researchers are working on deciphering whale-whale communication to better account for the impact of noise on this vulnerable population.

mix_items.title: Why global businesses are becoming quietly entwined with the military
mix_items.summary: Big corporations are not just influenced by governments anymore—they are being increasingly influenced by the military, according to a new study from the University of Surrey. The research, published in the Journal of World Business, shows many multinational firms now build competitive advantage through structured relationships with military institutions, from hiring veterans and using security contractors to accessing defense-funded innovations and protecting markets. Rather than being rare or controversial, this behavior is becoming routine in unstable global markets.

mix_items.title: Magnetic brain stimulation cuts alcohol cravings in 200-person addiction trial
mix_items.summary: A storyteller with a passion for popular education. Head of one of Linköping University's strongest research environments. And a physician. Markus Heilig researches the biology of the brain in addiction, and always considers the whole person.

mix_items.title: Europe advances genomic surveillance of antimicrobial resistance with multi-country study
mix_items.summary: A major European survey marks a significant milestone in the effort to control antimicrobial resistance (AMR), providing the most comprehensive genomic picture to date of carbapenem- and/or colistin-resistant Enterobacterales (CCRE) across hospitals in Europe. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales are critical priority pathogens posing a high risk for public health and patient safety, as they cause difficult-to-treat infections with limited available treatment options and an insufficient development pipeline for new antibiotics.

mix_items.title: Climate change is reshaping Europe's protected areas, and managers are adapting
mix_items.summary: New research shows how climate change is reshaping protected area management, though more funding and scientific knowledge are needed to facilitate the process. The Natura 2000 network, the world's largest network of protected areas, aims at protecting important species and habitats across the European Union. Its traditional approach to conservation has focused on maintaining ecosystems in their historical conditions, but in a world characterized by a changing climate, this may not be possible anymore, requiring adapting the conservation strategies.

mix_items.title: Barbell 'whip' may shape Olympic lifts more than lifters realize
mix_items.summary: In Olympic weightlifting, a single kilogram plate can be the difference between gold and silver. As much as possible, elite athletes must use everything they can to their advantage.

mix_items.title: Most weight loss achieved using injectable obesity drugs can be maintained by switching to orforglipron, study shows
mix_items.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May) and published in Nature Medicine shows that most weight loss achieved using the injectable obesity drugs tirzepatide or semaglutide can be maintained by switching to daily oral orforglipron treatment.

mix_items.title: Continuing tirzepatide at full dose helps preserve weight loss over 112 weeks
mix_items.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO 2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May) and published in The Lancet shows that people who have lost weight using the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of tirzepatide are seven times more likely to maintain that weight loss by continuing on MTD than those who stop treatment, while those who switch to a lower dose of 5 mg are four times more likely to maintain weight loss than those who stop.

mix_items.title: Predicting typhoon intensity using ocean surface temperatures
mix_items.summary: Every year in the West Pacific, as summer ends and September rolls around, typhoons are not far behind. Typhoons are the most impactful extreme weather events affecting Japan and East Asia, and due to climate change, extremely strong typhoons are becoming more frequent. In order to adapt critical infrastructure to these massive storms and protect coastal areas, accurate accounting for their future impact is essential.

mix_items.title: Laser treatment reshapes MOF pores, boosting CO₂ capture by up to 75%
mix_items.summary: A research team led by Hee-jung Lee, senior researcher at Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS), in collaboration with Professor Sunghwan Park of Kyungpook National University and Professor Mingyu Kim of Yeungnam University, has developed a technology that enhances CO₂ adsorption performance in metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) by up to 75% through precise laser-based control of their internal structure.

mix_items.title: A 13-gene panel may help predict response to chemotherapy in triple-negative breast cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center have characterized cancer cell-specific features in the tumor microenvironment (TME) of early-stage triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) tissues, identifying specific macrophage subtypes associated with chemotherapy response.

mix_items.title: A marine-inspired sunscreen ingredient made by E. coli
mix_items.summary: How do fish survive relentless sunlight in the open waters without getting burned? They make their own natural sunscreen—and now, humans could be one step closer to using it too.

mix_items.title: Non-coding gene is linked to core social and behavioral traits in autism
mix_items.summary: A long-overlooked stretch of the human genome appears to play a distinct role in shaping the social and stereotypic repetitive behaviors that define autism spectrum disorder (ASD), without affecting learning or other cognitive abilities, according to a study published in Nature.

mix_items.title: Identity traits sharply narrow who becomes friends or marries, model reveals
mix_items.summary: Our personal identity is composed of many dimensions, such as age, gender, ethnic background, or socioeconomic status. A research team led by Fariba Karimi from the Institute of Human-Centered Computing at Graz University of Technology (TU Graz) and Samuel Martin-Gutierrez from the Complexity Science Hub developed the statistical computational model "MAPS" to calculate the influence of these factors on our social relationships.

mix_items.title: SNOR protein provides 'all-clear' signal for dormant cells to resume normal operations
mix_items.summary: It's a tough world for microbes. When resources grow limited and environments worsen, microbes have figured out ways to hunker down and go dormant until conditions improve.

mix_items.title: 3D atomic rearrangement creates 40,000 quantum defects in 40 minutes
mix_items.summary: It's been 37 years since scientists first demonstrated the ability to move single atoms, suggesting the possibility of designing materials atom by atom to customize their properties. Today there are several techniques that allow researchers to move individual atoms in order to give materials exotic quantum properties and improve our understanding of quantum behavior.

mix_items.title: Pediatric surgery program cuts opioid use by 56%
mix_items.summary: A 21-element recovery program for children undergoing gastrointestinal surgery reduced opioid use during hospitalization by 56%, according to a large clinical trial led by Northwestern University and Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago.

mix_items.title: Fossil teeth from China uncover 400,000-year-old H. erectus ties to Denisovans
mix_items.summary: Scientists from the Institute of Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoanthropology (IVPP) of the Chinese Academy of Sciences have uncovered new information suggesting a potential connection between Homo erectus and modern humans, while also developing new, less invasive paleoproteomics methods of fossil research.

mix_items.title: AI generates first complete models of proteins in motion
mix_items.summary: Many drug and antibody discovery pathways focus on intricately folded cell membrane proteins. When molecules of a drug candidate bind to these proteins, like a key going into a lock, they trigger chemical cascades that alter cellular behavior. Understanding how proteins fold and move is therefore essential for developing drugs that interact well with their targets.

mix_items.title: Forgotten and isolated: 1.8 million people still vulnerable to COVID-19 face a mental health crisis
mix_items.summary: Levels of depression and anxiety remain high and largely unrecognized among an estimated 1.8 million clinically vulnerable people in the UK, many of whom continue to live significantly restricted lives to protect them from COVID 19, according to new research from the University of Bath.

mix_items.title: Rivalry with neighboring groups may be a key driver of male size in primates
mix_items.summary: In many primate species, males are much larger than their female counterparts, which is generally attributed to male competition for mates (sexual selection). But bigger bodies may not just be about alpha males defeating rivals. They could also come about because of competition between neighboring social groups, according to a new study published in the journal Biology Letters.

mix_items.title: This daily habit could lower dementia risk by 35%, scientists say
mix_items.summary: A huge long-term study found that drinking two to three cups of coffee a day was linked to a much lower risk of dementia, especially before age 75. Researchers say caffeine may help keep brain cells active while reducing inflammation and harmful plaque buildup associated with Alzheimer’s disease. But more coffee wasn’t better — the protective effect appeared to level off after moderate intake.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the 100-year mystery behind tough tires
mix_items.summary: For nearly 100 years, reinforced rubber has powered everything from car tires to airplanes, yet scientists never fully understood why adding tiny particles of carbon black made rubber so incredibly strong. Now, researchers at the University of South Florida have finally cracked the mystery using massive computer simulations that took the equivalent of 15 years of computing time. They discovered that carbon black forces rubber to “fight against itself” when stretched, dramatically boosting its strength and durability.

mix_items.title: Quantum breakthrough could revolutionize teleportation and computing
mix_items.summary: Scientists in Japan have developed a new way to instantly detect elusive quantum “W states,” a major milestone for quantum technology. The breakthrough could help unlock faster quantum communication, teleportation, and powerful new computing systems.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a mysterious silicone pollutant that may be everywhere
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered unexpectedly high levels of silicone-based pollutants called methylsiloxanes floating through the atmosphere across cities, rural regions, and even forests. Much of the pollution appears to come from vehicle emissions, likely linked to engine oil additives that survive combustion and escape into the air. Scientists say humans may inhale more of these compounds daily than other notorious pollutants like PFAS or microplastics.

mix_items.title: Your “um” and pauses could reveal early dementia risk
mix_items.summary: The little pauses, “ums,” and moments when you struggle to find the right word may reveal far more about your brain than anyone realized. Researchers discovered that everyday speech patterns are closely tied to executive function — the mental system that powers memory, planning, focus, and flexible thinking. By using AI to analyze natural conversations, the team found they could predict cognitive performance with surprising accuracy, potentially opening the door to simple speech-based tools that could detect early signs of dementia long before traditional testing does.

mix_items.title: Scientists found the brain doesn’t start blank, it starts full
mix_items.summary: The brain’s memory center may begin life more like a crowded web than an empty canvas. Researchers discovered that early neural networks in the hippocampus are dense and seemingly random, then become more organized by shedding connections over time. This pruning process creates a faster, more efficient system for linking experiences and forming memories. It challenges the idea that the brain starts from scratch.

mix_items.title: Are your memories real? Physicists revisit the Boltzmann brain paradox
mix_items.summary: A new analysis of the “Boltzmann brain” paradox suggests our memories and sense of reality could, in theory, be random illusions born from cosmic chaos. By uncovering circular reasoning in how physicists think about time and entropy, the study raises fresh doubts about what we can truly know about the past.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Cafeaua mea are acum rol de team lead.

satire_ro.title: Restructurarea STB provoacă haos după ce mai mulți vatmani au fost dați afară din mers
